coredata.dc:title: Synergistic doped and loaded strategies to modify WO3 nanoflowers for efficient photocatalytic degradation of antibiotics through reactive oxygen species regulation

coredata.dc:description: Developing an effective and powerful photocatalyst is important to its practical application in water treatment area, like emerging contaminants removal in water. Herein, through a synergistic strategy of N-doping and carbon quantum dots (CQDs)-loading on WO3 nanoflowers, CQDs/N-WO3 was designed, which exhibited excellent photocatalytic performance due to modulated electronic structure. Both experimental characterization and theoretical calculation confirmed that the synergistic effect accelerated the electron transfer ability from O2p to W5d, thus promoting the photogenerated electron and hole separation efficiency owing to the increased W5d orbital electron density. The optimum photocatalyst achieved 100 % removal of gatifloxacin (GAT) with a high reaction rate constant (k 1) of 0.055 min−1 in 60 min under simulated solar light. Moreover, quenching experiment and radical detection demonstrated that the main reactive oxygen species (ROS) changed from •OH (WO3 and N-WO3) to O2 •− (CQDs/N-WO3) for GAT removal. Density functional theory (DFT) calculation based on Fukui index further indicated the reactive sites of GAT were available for electrophilic attack. The internal mechanism was also deduced that after N doped and CQDs loaded on WO3, narrowed band gap and increased utilization of visible light were observed. Moreover, the high electron density of N sites was more conducive to hole capture, and CQDs enhanced the electron transport as electron acceptor and transporter. The synergistic strategy of doping and loading opens a new perspective for modulating the electronic structure of catalysts, and provides a reference for regulating the ROS types in Fenton-like reactions for the removal of emerging contaminants in water.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Developing an effective and powerful photocatalyst is important to its practical application in water treatment area, like emerging contaminants removal in water. Herein, through a synergistic strategy of N-doping and carbon quantum dots (CQDs)-loading on WO3 nanoflowers, CQDs/N-WO3 was designed, which exhibited excellent photocatalytic performance due to modulated electronic structure. Both experimental characterization and theoretical calculation confirmed that the synergistic effect accelerated the electron transfer ability from O2p to W5d, thus promoting the photogenerated electron and hole separation efficiency owing to the increased W5d orbital electron density. The optimum photocatalyst achieved 100 % removal of gatifloxacin (GAT) with a high reaction rate constant (k 1) of 0.055 min−1 in 60 min under simulated solar light. Moreover, quenching experiment and radical detection demonstrated that the main reactive oxygen species (ROS) changed from •OH (WO3 and N-WO3) to O2 •− (CQDs/N-WO3) for GAT removal. Density functional theory (DFT) calculation based on Fukui index further indicated the reactive sites of GAT were available for electrophilic attack. The internal mechanism was also deduced that after N doped and CQDs loaded on WO3, narrowed band gap and increased utilization of visible light were observed. Moreover, the high electron density of N sites was more conducive to hole capture, and CQDs enhanced the electron transport as electron acceptor and transporter. The synergistic strategy of doping and loading opens a new perspective for modulating the electronic structure of catalysts, and provides a reference for regulating the ROS types in Fenton-like reactions for the removal of emerging contaminants in water.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Quinolone antibiotics, as an important component of the pharmaceuticals and personal care products (PPCPs) [1,2], were a kind of drugs commonly used in humans and animals. Due to the antibacterial spectrum is broad, exhibiting robust antibacterial activity, with no cross-resistance observed with other antimicrobial agents and minimal side effects [3], it was extensively employed in humanhealth, animal husbandry, aquatic products and other aquaculture for prevention and treatment of their diseases. Among them, gatifloxacin (GAT) was one of the most common new fourth generation fluoroquinolones (FQS). The antibiotic exhibited a wide spectrum of activity, effectively targeting both Gram-positive and Gram-negative bacteria. Frustratingly, adsorbing only small portions in human and low biodegradability, a majority of GAT was finally excreted into aquatic environments through metabolism [4,5]. Thedetectionoftracelevel GAT in surface water [6], GAT may exhibited unexpected hazards to human health and aquatic ecosystems [7,8]. As an illustration, GAT was prone to damage the function of the cardiovascular system, and the mechanism was likely due to the down-regulation of calcium signaling pathway and myocardial contraction-related genes [9]. Unfortunately, conventional wastewater treatment processes did not eliminate it effectively. Therefore, due to the increase in concern related to water quality and public health, it was highly desirable and urgent to develop environmentally friendly techniques for GAT removal. Photocatalysis as one kind of advanced oxidation processes (AOPs) has been regarded as the energy-saving, environmental protection, and green technology under solar irradiation to eliminate the organic pollutants from wastewater [10–12]. In particular, heterogeneous photocatalysis was a possibly environmentally friendly disinfection and advanced oxidation process that did not require the continuous addition of chemical disinfectants and attracted a lot of attention, among which common catalysts are carbon nitride (C3N4), titanium dioxide (TiO2), and tungsten oxide (WO3) [13–15]. So, it was particularly important to develop and prepare a simple, efficient and economical photocatalytic material.

Among the various semiconductor photocatalysts, tungsten oxide (WO3), owing to its inherent characteristics such as low cost, environmentfriendly, proper band gap, excellent electron transport properties, and moderate hole diffusion length, was considered to be a promising visible light-driven photocatalysts in solar energy conversion and environmental remediation [16–18]. Many strategies, including thermodynamic (doping with heteroatoms [19], engineering defects with oxygen vacancies or tungsten vacancies [20,21], etc.) and kinetic (coupling with other semiconductors, morphology design, surface modification, and loading cocatalysts, etc.) modifications [22], have emerged to enhance the photocatalytic degradation function of WO3 [23,24]. Doping was an important way to improve the light energy conversion efficiency of photocatalytic materials, some nonmetal doping improved the electrical conductivity and narrowed the band gap of semiconductor materials [25]. For example, the dopant was introduced into the WO3 framework to improve the light absorption capacity and promote the charge carrier transfer kinetics by designing the energy band structure and electronic structure, thus the photocatalytic activity was significantly enhanced [26]. Therefore, N doping was considered as an effective doping that helpful in enhancing the photocatalytic degradation activity of WO3. Moreover, carbon quantum dots (CQDs) typically consisted of quasi-spherical nanoparticles [27]. The conjugated structure of CQDs enables their application in various exciting fields, including sensor technology, bioimaging, catalysis, and photovoltaic devices [28–31]. Due to their excellent electron transfer-reservoir properties, CQDs have recently been successfully incorporated into semiconductors (e.g., TiO2 [32,33], Cu2O [34], Ag3PO4 [35], ZnO [36], BiVO4 [37]) to enhance photocatalytic performance. However, there is a lack of systematic studies on the relationship between structure and photocatalytic activity for CQDs-based hybrid materials. Furthermore, the mechanism underlying pollutant photodegradation by these materials remains to be further explored. According to the advantages of WO3, N doping and CQDs loading described above, combining these materials together may result in a hybrid structure with enhanced photocatalytic performance.

In this study, CQDs modified N-WO3 catalyst (CQDs/N-WO3) was synthesized via the solvothermal method and calcination, which was used for boosting photocatalytic degradation of GAT. The specific objectives of this work were: (1) to analyze the composition, crystal structure, and energy band structure of the catalysts based on a series of characterizations; (2) to reveal the effect of N doping and CQDs introduction on the structure and internal mechanism based on density functional theory (DFT) calculations; (3) to evaluate the photocatalytic degradation performance of GAT under simulated solar irradiation and investigate the photocatalysis behavior of GAT under light of different wavelengths; (4) to identify the reactive oxygen species participated in GAT photocatalytic degradation process by trapping experiments and the electron spin resonance (ESR) technique; (5) to explore the degradation pathway for GAT and use DFT calculations to interpret the mechanism by which reactive oxygen species (ROS) attacks GAT. This work not only supported a theoretical basis for modulating the catalyst structure through doping and loading strategies, but also provided a reference for degrading wastewater containing fluoroquinolone antibiotics.
The chemicals and reagents utilized in this work are detailed in Text S1 of the Supplementary information (SI).

Preparation of CQDs: The CQDs were prepared according to a previous report [38]. Briefly, 6.3042 g citric acid monohydrate was dissolved in 60 mL deionized water, then 2.01 mL ethylenediamine was added. The above solution was transferred into a polytetrafluoroethylene-lined reactor and heated at 200 °C for 5 h. After natural cooling naturally to room temperature, the resulting dark brown CQDs solution was stored at 4 °C in a refrigerator for follow-up experiments.

Preparation of N-WO3: The sample was prepared via solvothermal process with 0.04 g of ammonium fluoride (NH4F) and 0.4 g (WCl6) tungsten chloride as tungsten source into ethanol (25 mL) with stirring to obtain the white solution at room temperature. And then the solution was added into 50 mL capacity of Teflon-lined stainless steel autoclave with heating at 150 °C for 5 h. After cooling down to room temperature, the blue products washed with absolute ethanol by centrifuging and dried at 60 °C. Finally, the blue samples were annealed in air at 550 °C in a muffle furnace for 1h to get yellow products of WO3 [15,39]. Then 200 mg WO3 and 40 mg urea were mixed and ground for 15 min in a mortar. The appeal powder was put into a tube furnace at 200 °C for 2 h, and then the powder namely N-WO3 was obtained.

Preparation of CQDs/N-WO3: The composites were prepared by blending and calcination. The preparation process was as follows: 0.2 g of the prepared N-WO3 was dispersed in 50 mL deionized water under magnetic stirring. Then add a certain amount of CQDs (dropwise addition of 2 mL) solution. After stirring continuously at room temperature for 12 h, the mixture was subjected to centrifugation at 8000 rpm for 5 min, followed by three washes with deionized water. Then, the product was dried at 105 °C for 12 h. Then the brown powder namely CQDs/N-WO3 was successfully synthesized.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Experimental and analytical methods of GAT photocatalytic degradation
ce:section.ce:para: The solar simulator (Microsolar300A, 300 W Xe arc lamp, 100 mW/cm2) was used as the light source in this experiment. The constant temperature of the photocatalytic reactor was 25 °C. In each experiment, the 100 mL GAT (10 μmol/L) solution was placed in the quartz glass reactor. Then the 10 mg photocatalyst was suspended into GAT solution, and the initial pH of the solution was about 5.5 (except pH effect experiment). Thedarkreactionprocess was maintained for 0.5 h to realize adsorption–desorption equilibrium. As a contrast, a blank control experiment was carried out without photocatalyst. Besides, to explore the photocatalytic activity of CQDs/N-WO3 at different light wavelengths, a series of experiments on the photocatalytic degradation of GAT were conducted under irradiation of 365, 385, 420, 450, 485, 520, 595, 630 nm light and standard white light (400–800 nm). A multichannel photochemical reaction system (PCX50C, PerfectLight, China) with nine light-emitting diodes (LED) was used with irradiation intensity fixed at 10 mW/cm2.

To comprehensively investigate the influence of pH on the photocatalytic degradation process, the diluted HClO4 or NaOH were employed to precisely adjust the pH of the solution to predetermined values of 3.0, 5.5, 7.0, 9.0, and 11.0. The wide applicability of catalysts in acidic, neutral and alkaline water environments was investigated. Following the pH adjustments, photocatalytic degradation experiments using GAT were conducted at each pH level to assess the impact on the reaction's efficiency.

To better understand the specific reactive oxygen species (ROS) involved in the photocatalytic degradation of GAT by the composite materials, a series of quenching experiments were performed. These experiments aimed to identify the dominant ROS facilitating the degradation process. Three quenching agents were used: Tiron (TI) was used to selectively quench superoxide radicals (O2 •−), isopropanol (IPA) was used to quench hydroxyl radicals (•OH), and potassium iodide (KI) was used to capture photogenerated holes (h+). The experimental procedure was as follows: First, 100 mL of GAT (10 μmol/L) was added to a quartz reactor, and the solution's pH was adjusted to 5.5. Next, 10 mmol/L of the selected quencher was introduced and stirred for 3 min to make the solution evenly mixed. Subsequently, 10 mg of the catalyst was added to the reactor. At specific time intervals, 1 mL of the reaction mixture was extracted and filtered with 0.22 μm PTFE membrane. The filtrate was analyzed and the GAT concentration was determined. By systematically quenching each ROS, this study aimed to evaluate the relative contributions of superoxide radicals, hydroxyl radicals, and photogenerated holes in the photocatalytic degradation of GAT. The findings are expected to clarify the specific ROS mechanisms involved and enhance the understanding of how the composite material facilitates photocatalytic activity[30,40].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Structural characterization of the synthesized catalysts
ce:section.ce:para:
The synthesis procedure for the CQDs/N-WO3 composite is illustrated schematically in Fig. 1
a. The physicochemical properties of commercial WO3 and CQDs/N-WO3 were initially analyzed using scanning electron microscopy (SEM). As shown in Fig. S1 , the commercial WO3 exhibited small nanoparticle structures. When CQDs were introduced into N-WO3, the nanoflower-like structures were found as depicted in Fig. 1 b. To investigate the microstructure of the catalysts in detail, transmission electron microscopy (TEM) was employed as shown in Fig. 1 c. The results demonstrated that the edge of nanoflower-like CQDs/N-WO3 was the morphology of petals. High-resolution TEM (HRTEM) analysis (Fig. 1 d) further revealed small black dots, highlighted within purple circles, corresponding to CQDs. Additionally, yellow-marked lattice fringes were observed, corresponding to the (020) crystal plane of monoclinic tungsten oxide (JCPDS No. 43–1035) with a lattice fringe spacing of 0.377 nm[10,41]. Energy dispersive X-ray spectroscopy (EDS) elemental mapping (Fig. 1 e–1i) demonstrated the uniform distribution of W, O, N, and C elements within the CQDs/N-WO3.

Detailed information about the catalyst characterization is provided in Text S2 . The powder X-ray diffraction (XRD) patterns of WO3, N-WO3, and CQDs/N-WO3 are shown in Fig. 2
a. The diffraction peaks of WO3, represented by the blue line in Fig. 2 a, correspond well to monoclinic WO3 (JCPDS No. 43–1035). Additionally, the sharpness of the peaks indicates the high crystallinity of the WO3 [42]. The main peaks observed at 2θ = 23.12°, 23.58° and 24.39° correspond to the (002), (020) and (200) crystal planes of monoclinic WO3 [43]. In addition, the XRD patterns of WO3 (blue line), N-WO3 (purple line), and CQDs/N-WO3 (pink line) showed no characteristic diffraction peaks from other phases, indicating that the synergistic strategy of N doping and CQDs loading did not alter the crystal structure of WO3 [44]. Furthermore, X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) analysis was performed to confirm the composition and oxidation states of the elements in the CQDs/N-WO3 composite. The survey spectrum in Fig. 2 b clearly shows prominent photoelectron peaks for W 4f and O 1 s, along with peaks for the elements W, O, N, and C, with no peaks from other elements. This confirms the high purity of the synthesized catalyst. The high-resolution W 4f spectrum, displayed in Fig. 2 c, reveals split peaks at 35.85 eV for WO3, 35.65 eV for N-WO3, and 35.60 eV for CQDs/N-WO3, all of which correspond to the W (VI) oxidation state in the W 4f7/2 region[45–47]. The cleavage peaks of WO3 at 37.95 eV, N-WO3 at 37.81 eV and CQDs/N-WO3 at 37.73 eV were all classified as W (VI) of W4f5/2. Compared to WO3 (blue curve), the N-WO3 (purple curve) showed that the splitting peak of W 4f7/2 shifted 0.20 eV towards the lower binding energy after doping N, while the splitting peak of W 4f7/2 of CQDs/N-WO3 shifted 0.25 eV towards the lower binding energy. It suggested that N doped and CQDs loaded increased the electron cloud density near the W atoms in both N-WO3 and CQDs/N-WO3 [20]. In the O 1 s spectra shown in Fig. 2 d, the primary peak at 530.67 eV was assigned to lattice oxygen (OL) or coordinated oxygen in WO3 (W-O) [20,48,49]. For CQDs/N-WO3, additional peaks at 530.41 eV and 531.49 eV corresponded to W-O and adsorbed oxygen (e.g., O from H2O), respectively [50]. A comparison of lattice oxygen binding energies revealed that, after N doping and CQDs loading, WO3 shifted to a lower binding energy, indicating that these modifications did not disrupt the stability of the WO3 structure. In the C 1 s spectra (Fig. 2 e), the main peaks for CQDs/N-WO3 at 284.81 eV and 286.79 eV were attributed to C-C and C-N bonds[42], confirming the successful synthesis of the material. Fig. 2 f showed that the binding energy of N 1 s in both N-WO3 and CQDs/N-WO3 shifted negatively by 1.25 eV, suggesting that CQDs enhanced the electron trapping ability in the composite. Two additional peaks at 399.99 eV and 398.74 eV were attributed to N-O and C-N-O bonds[51,52].

As illustrated in Fig. 3
a, the optical properties of the prepared catalyst were analyzed by diffuse reflectance spectroscopy (UV–vis DRS). It was observed that the absorption edges of WO3, N-WO3, and CQDs/N-WO3 were 420 nm, 433.5 nm, and 442.5 nm, respectively. Therefore, it was demonstrated that the ability of the composite to capture electrons in the visible region was enhanced with the N doped and CQDs loaded. In addition, it was evident that CQDs/N-WO3 had wide absorption in the range of visible light. The relationship between (αhν)1/2 and (hv) photon energy of the catalysts was depicted in Fig. 3 b, according to the Kubelka-Munk function. This plot revealed the band gap energy spectra of WO3, N-WO3 and CQDs/N-WO3, and the observed transformations were related to their electronic structures and band gap energy (E g). The E g of a semiconductor a semiconductor could be evaluated from the following equation:
(1)
( α h ν )
1 / n
= A
(
E g
- h ν )
where α is the absorption coefficient, hν represent photon energy, A is a proportionality constant. The WO3 semiconductor possesses an indirect electron transition, and so n = 2 [53]. Therefore, it was found that the band gap of WO3 was 2.95 eV, the valence band potential of N-WO3 was 2.86 eV, and that of CQDs/N-WO3 composites was 2.80 eV. Compared to WO3 and CQDs/N-WO3, the band gap of the composite decreased from 2.95 eV to 2.80 eV. This decrease indicated that the oxidation ability of h+ in the valence band of the composite, when excited by photons, was stronger than that of WO3, thus enhancing the light absorption capability of CQDs/N-WO3. For the CQDs/N-WO3 composite, under visible light irradiation, electrons from the conduction band of N-WO3 transferred to the CQDs, generating an internal electric field that mitigated electron-hole recombination and improved the photocatalytic performance of the composite. In the valence band spectra XPS (VB-XPS) spectra (Fig. 3 c), the VB position displayed a markedly negative shift from WO3 (2.88 eV) to CQDs/N-WO3 (2.42 eV), suggesting an upward VB position with a shift value of 0.46 eV. Based on the E g values, the conduction band (CB) edges were determined to be − 0.07 eV for WO3 and − 0.38 eV for CQDs/N-WO3, as shown in Fig. 3 d. The upward shift of the VB in CQDs/N-WO3 promoted hole mobility, leading to efficient electron-hole separation and enhanced radical production, as previously reported [54–56]. Besides, the conduction band minimum (CBM) of CQDs/N-WO3 needed to be more negative than the reduction potential of E 0(O2/O2 •−) (−0.33 eV vs. normal hydrogen electrode (NHE)) [57], and the valence band maximum (VBM) should be more positive than the oxidation potential of E 0(•OH/OH−) (2.40 eV vs. NHE) [18,40,58]. Thus, the CQDs/N-WO3 could produce O2 •−, which was confirmed by subsequent experiments.

The DFT calculation was performed to deeply investigate the underlying improved photocatalytic mechanism resulting from N doping and CQDs loading [59], with the relevant structural models displayed in Fig. 4
a-4c. The projected density of states (PDOS) spectra of WO3 in Fig. 4 d presented the W5d and O2p orbitals primarily contributed to the CB and VB, respectively. In contrast, the PDOS of N-WO3 in Fig. 4 e displayed N2p also contributed to both the VB and CB near the Fermi level, compared to WO3.This indicated that the electronic structure was modified after N doping. The introduction of N narrowed the relevant band gap and facilitated electrons transfer from O2p to W5d. Moreover, the high electron density of N sites, as shown in PDOS in Fig. 4 e, promoted hole trapping. Then, Fig. 4 f exhibited electrons states of W5d orbital was moved closer to near the Fermi level due to the CQDs loaded, indicating electrons transfer energy barrier decreased, and electrons activity was enhanced. In addition, it can be noticed that electrons in all orbitals became more delocalized compared to WO3 and N-WO3, indicating the electrical conductivity was improved after loading CQDs, thus the band gap was further narrowed, and electrons transfer efficiency greatly promoted compared with WO3 and N-WO3. Besides, it could be found that O2p, C2p and N2p contributed to VB, and C2p and W5d contributed to the CB in Fig. 4 f, meaning that the electrons transfer direction was O2p → N2p → C2p → W5d under the simulated solar light. This transfer process not only accelerated electrons transfer rate, but also hindered the generated carrier’s recombination. Furthermore, the d band center of CQDs/N-WO3 (– 0.36 eV) in Fig. 4 f was closer to the Fermi level than other catalysts, suggesting electrons density and utilization rate increased in W5d. Thus, based on the PDOS analysis, CQDs/N-WO3 possessed the best electrons transfer efficiency, band gap, electrons utilization and carriers’ recombination due to the introduction of N and CQDs, further it has an excellent photocatalytic performance. Then, the theoretical band gap was calculated in Figs. S2-S4
, which was 1.87 eV, 0.53 eV and 0.26 eV, further proving CQDs/N-WO3 possessed the narrowest band gap and photocatalytic reactivity. Meanwhile, the acquired band gap was lower than the practical values due to the known limitations of the plain DFT method. Moreover, Fig. S5 also presented the W atom in CQDs/N-WO3 had the strongest electron density, further the electronic reactivity of W5d and photocatalytic performance were enhanced. In addition, the work function in Fig. 4 g-4i suggested the electrons transfer direction was from CQDs to N to WO3. This suggested that WO3 could be coupled with an electronic storage device, allowing for more efficient electron utilization during the photocatalytic process compared to the other two catalysts.

The photocatalytic activity for degradation of GAT by developed materials under simulated solar light was further evaluated, as shown in Fig. 5
a. Prior to the photocatalytic degradation experiments and test methods in Text S3 , a blank test without any photocatalyst was conducted, confirming that direct photolysis of GAT was negligible. The CQDs/N-WO3 composite exhibited the highest photocatalytic performance, achieving a 100 % removal rate of GAT within 60 min. The corresponding pseudo-first-order reaction rate constant (k 1) of the WO3, N-WO3, and CQDs/N-WO3 was exhibited in Fig. 5 b. The k 1 for WO3 was 0.006 min−1, and for N-WO3 was 0.015 min−1.These results indicated that nitrogen doping slightly enhanced the degradation efficiency of GAT, consistent with previous studies [25,26,42]. Surprisingly, the k 1 for GAT degradation in the CQDs/N-WO3 system was 0.055 min−1, approximately 9.2 times higher than that of WO3 alone. The result indicated that CQDs effectively served as active sites for the reaction, playing a crucial role in enhancing the photocatalytic activity. Furthermore, the photocatalytic performance of CQDs/N-WO3 for GAT degradation under different wavelengths of light is shown in Fig. 5 c. Nine wavelengths of light were used as the radiation source in the experiment in Text S4 . The results clearly showed that the degradation of GAT was closely related to the wavelength of light. Specifically, 80.2 % of GAT was degraded under visible light within 60 min. Moreover, UV light at 365 nm (UV365) and 385 nm (UV385) completely degraded GAT in 60 min, indicating that CQDs/N-WO3 exhibited strong responsiveness to ultraviolet light. Purple light (420 nm) also achieved complete degradation of GAT, but required approximately 75 min. In contrast, orange light (595 nm) and red light (630 nm) inhibited the degradation of GAT, with degradation rates of 19.40 % and 11.14 %, respectively, after 75 min. To further investigate the effect of different initial pH values on the photocatalytic degradation of GAT, pH experiments were conducted at pH values of 3.0, 5.5, 7.0, 9.0, and 11.0 under simulated solar light. As shown in Fig. 5 d, the degradation rate of GAT was fastest at pH 5.5, with complete degradation occurring within 60 min. In contrast, the degradation of GAT was inhibited to varying degrees under acidic (pH 3) and alkaline (pH 11) conditions, indicating that strong acidic or basic environments were not favorable for the photocatalytic degradation of GAT. Furthermore, as the pH increased from 5.5 to 11, the inhibitory effect on GAT degradation became more pronounced. GAT removal at different pH was closely related to the charged surface of CQDs/N-WO3 and species of GAT. At different pH, GAT existed in different dissociation forms because of the two pKa values (pKa1 = 5.7 and pKa2 = 9.0). Especially, at pH 5.7 − 9.0, GAT existed in the zwitterionic form and GAT molecules (GAT+, GAT, and GAT−) [60,61]. The negatively charged CQDs/N-WO3 as depicted in Fig. S6 readily captured GAT+ at acidic conditions (Fig. 5 e), thus promoting the subsequent reaction in accordance with previous studies [62]. Furthermore, an increased number of negatively charged sites on the surface of CQDs/N-WO3 at higher pH inhibited material aggregation due to electrostatic repulsion[63]. Besides, given that WO3 was not stable and would transform into tungstate at high pH because of the reaction (WO3 + 2OH−→WO4 2−+ H2O). Therefore, CQDs/N-WO3 exhibited high photocatalytic activity for GAT degradation at pH 5.5.

To evaluate the impact of different water quality conditions on the photocatalytic degradation of GAT, water quality influence experiments were conducted. The experiments were carried out without pH adjustment and without pretreatment of the raw water, as shown in Fig. 5 f. The results indicated that the best degradation of GAT was achieved using ultrapure water, with 100 % degradation within 60 min. Under the conditions of tap water (TW), river water (RW), and lake water (LW), the degradation effect was slightly inhibited, but approximately 90 % degradation of GAT was still achieved within 60 min under simulated solar light irradiation. These results demonstrated that CQDs/N-WO3 exhibited good adaptability to different water qualities. To assess the degradation performance of CQDs/N-WO3 on various types of antibiotics, degradation experiments were performed under the same conditions (UW, pH 5.5, Catalyst = 0.1 g/L, Cp = 0.01 mM, simulated solar light). Four typical antibiotics, namely moxifloxacin hydrochloride (MXF), norfloxacin (NOR), tetracycline (TC), and GAT, were selected. The degradation rate constants (k1 ) and structures of the four antibiotics are presented in Fig. 5 g. NOR exhibited the fastest degradation rate with 100 % removal, while TC showed a degradation efficiency of 85 %. The degradation efficiencies of the four antibiotics are shown in Fig. S7 . These results indicated that CQDs/N-WO3 effectively degraded a variety of antibiotics, demonstrating its broad applicability. The effects of anions and cations participation for the GAT degradation were also considered in Fig. 5 h. With the exception of Cl–, the presence of other ions had minimal impact on the GAT removal rate within 60 min. And in the presence of Cl–, the degradation rate reached 90.58 %, the reason for this result was the competitive adsorption between Cl–, GAT, and catalyst, which hindered the photocatalytic degradation process [64,65]. Furthermore, the reusability of the CQDs/N-WO3 photocatalyst was tested by performing photocatalytic degradation of GAT under simulated solar light over five cycles, as shown in Fig. S8 . The results indicated that the removal efficiency remained as high as 90.48 % after the fifth cycle, demonstrating the excellent reusability of CQDs/N-WO3. Finally, the total organic carbon (TOC) removal efficiency of the CQDs/N-WO3 photocatalytic system was evaluated in Fig. 5 i. At 10 min, the TOC removal rate was only 26.32 %, which increased to 35.85 % at 20 min and reached 42.48 % after 60 min. The results indicated that the system could effectively mineralize the degraded intermediates into H2O and CO2, and complete mineralization could be achieved by further prolonging the reaction time.

To explore the role of reactive oxygen species (ROS) in photocatalytic degradation of GAT, a quenching experiment was conducted using different catalysts under simulated solar light, and the results were depicted in Fig. 6 . Tiron, KI and IPA were introduced into the system to quench superoxide free radicals (O2 •–) [66], holes (h+) [67]and hydroxyl radicals (•OH) [15], respectively. In the ROS quenching experiment for the WO3 and N-WO3 systems, the addition of Tiron had minimal effect on GAT degradation efficiency (Fig. 6 a and 6b), suggesting that O2 •– was not the primary ROS involved. However, the addition of KI and IPA significantly inhibited the degradation efficiency of GAT, indicating that h+ and •OH played dominant roles. While the quenching experiment for CQDs/N-WO3 system demonstrated that the addition of Tiron and KI had the greatest effect on the GAT degradation (Fig. 6 c), indicating that O2 •– and h+ played a prominent role in photocatalysis process. Nevertheless, the inhibitory effect on GAT degradation was not significant, indicating that the role of •OH could be neglected. Thus, ROS for WO3 and N-WO3 systems were h+ and •OH, while that for CQDs/N-WO3 systems were O2 •− and h+. Electron spin resonance (ESR) analysis was further employed to detect the generation of •OH and O2 •− in the CQDs/N-WO3 system with the 5, 5-dimethyl- L-pyrroline N-oxide (DMPO) as the spin-trapping agents [23]. For the detection of ROS in WO3 and N-WO3 systems, under solar light irradiation (Fig. 6 d), four peaks of the characteristic spectra of DMPO-•OH adducts were found [68], and the hyperfine splitting constant was α N = α H = 14.9 G with an intensity ratio of 1:2:2:1 [69], which was consistent with the experiment of radicals quenching. While for the CQDs/N-WO3 system, no signals of DMPO-•OH adducts were detected ( Fig. S9 ). For the O2 •− detection results in Fig. 6 e, typical peaks of DMPO-O2 •− were observed under simulated solar light, indicating that O2 •− was generated during the photocatalytic process, which was confirmed by the radical quenching experiment (Fig. 6 c). And the capture experiments of O2 •– was quantitatively analyzed at different time intervals (2 min and 5 min) under the same conditions. As shown in Fig. 6 e, the peak intensity was lower at 2 min, indicating a smaller number of O2 •– present in the system. However, at 5 min, a significantly higher intensity peak was observed, suggesting the presence of a large quantity of O2 •– during the reaction system.

Based on the results of quenching experiments and ESR analysis, the contribution rate of active species in WO3, N-WO3, and CQDs/N-WO3 system was drawn in Fig. 6 f. Among them, the removal efficiency of GAT was 14.76 % and 25.36 % in the WO3 and N-WO3 systems after the addition of IPA to quenched •OH, respectively, indicating that •OH was the main ROS in the above two systems. In the CQDs/N-WO3 system, the removal efficiency of GAT was only 45.68 % after the addition of Tiron, suggesting that the contribution of O2 •− was approximately 54.32 %. However, with the addition of IPA, the GAT removal rate was 89.32 %, indicating that the contribution of •OH was minimal and almost negligible. Thus, the ROS was regulated from •OH (WO3 and N-WO3) to O2 •− (CQDs/N-WO3) for antibiotic removal. To explore the electron transport characteristics in the catalysts the linear sweep voltammetry (LSV) and electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) was performed. LSV results in Fig. S10 demonstrated that the CQDs/N-WO3 electrode exhibited a higher total current density in the potential range compared with WO3 and N-WO3, suggesting its superior electron transport capacity [70]. Besides, the EIS analysis of WO3, N-WO3, and CQDs/N-WO3 was also conducted to investigate the electron transfer process as presented in Fig. S11 . In the Nyquist plots, the semicircle radius reflected the charge transfer rate at the interface between the working electrode and the electrolyte solution. The semicircle radius of CQDs/N-WO3 catalyst was the smallest, indicating that the least charge transfer resistance and the highest electron transfer efficiency [71].

ce:section.ce:section-title: DFT calculations of GAT, possible degradation pathway
ce:section.ce:para:
The possible sites of GAT during ROS attack were predicted theoretically by using DFT calculation. Fig. 7
a-7c displayed the GAT model, the highest occupied molecular orbital (HOMO) and the lowest unoccupied molecular orbital (LUMO) orbitals. Generally, HOMO and LUMO represented the ability of lose and acquired electrons, respectively. Fig. 7 b and Fig. 7 c obviously displayed the HOMO and LUMO mainly gathered at two benzene rings, which can be more easily attacked via electrophilic species. Due to the generation of O2 •– belonged to the electrophiles, thus, it may mainly attack two benzene rings of GAT. Furthermore, natural population analysis (NPA) charge populations and Fukui index distribution for electrophilic attack (f
− ) of GAT molecule has been exhibited in Fig. 7 d. The Fukui index (f − ) was used to analyze the active sites of GAT for O2 •– attack, and detailed calculation methods were given in Text S5 . The high f – of C1 (f − = 0.06365), C9 (f − = 0.15456), and N26 (f − = 0.1077) may be mainly attacked by O2 •–. Then, the transformation products (TPs) were explored by applying liquid chromatography-mass spectrometer (LC-MS) in Fig. S12 and Text S6 . The GAT degradation was summarized into three pathways in Fig. 7 e. For the pathway I, which was undergoing F bond cleavage, after the C1 site was attacked by ROS, it had a high f value (f − =0.06365), resulting in a major product P1 m/z = 251.05 [72]. Then P1 was further broken to form P2 m/z = 194.03, then P2 was further oxidized to P3 m/z = 179.02. Pathway II was initiated by the destruction of the cyclopropyl group and the F atom was replaced by hydroxyl to form P4 m/z = 349.13 [73]. The P4 intermediate was then reacted to form P5 m/z = 323.10 [74], followed by ring opening to form P6 m/z = 207.05. Pathway III: It was initiated by the electrophilic addition of ROS, which lead to the double bond cleavage of the quinolone part, resulting in the formation of P7 m/z = 411.15 [48,52]. The P7 intermediate was further decarboxylated and the hydroxyl group on the F atom replaced and destroyed quinolone and benzene ring to form P8 m/z = 305.14. The P8 intermediate was further decarboxylated to form P9 m/z = 293.05, and then P9 was further opened to form P10 m/z = 235.07 [72,75]. Finally, the small molecules P3, P6 and P10 were further oxidized to CO2 and H2O or small acid.

Additionally, the developmental toxicity of the intermediates/products resulting from GAT degradation was assessed using the Toxicity Estimation Software Tool (T.E.S.T.) through quantitative structure–activity relationship (QSAR) prediction. As shown in Fig. 7 f, only one intermediate (P9 m/z = 293.05) of the developmental toxicity was higher than the GAT. Catalytic degradation of GAT under simulated solar light resulted in eight intermediates that were all less toxic than GAT, while P3 (m/z = 179.02) intermediates were even non-toxic and harmless. The noteworthy aspect is that the majority of the products consist of defluorinated compounds, thereby significantly mitigating the toxicity of pollutants. It was also observed that a five-membered ring was formed on the piperazine ring during the oxidation of fluoroquinolone. By further prolonging the reaction time, the intermediates were then oxidized to smaller intermediates, mineralizing to form CO2 and H2O.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Internal mechanism of photocatalytic degradation of GAT by different catalysts
ce:section.ce:para:
The photocatalytic mechanism for the WO3, N-WO3, and CQDs/N-WO3 under simulated solar light irradiation was summarized in Fig. 8 . In the CQDs/N-WO3 system, the N-WO3 in the composite produced electron-hole pairs under light irradiation, and the photoexcited electron (e −) transitioned from the VB to CB, remaining the hole (h+) in VB (Eq.
(2)
[76]. Based on the DFT calculations, the generated e – in VB (O2p orbitals) initially transferred to N2p and then transferred to C2p. The C2p, acting as a bridge, connected to W5d, facilitating further e − transfer to W5d. This process enhanced electron transfer efficiency and inhibited carrier recombination. Then, the conjugated structure and good electrical conductivity of CQDs in the composite allowed for efficient electron transfer, further reducing electron-hole recombination and improving photocatalytic activity. Moreover, CQDs also played a role in scavenging excited electrons from the VB, thereby further inhibiting electron-hole recombination [48,77,78]. Owing to the reduction potential of E 0(O2/O2 •−) (−0.33 eV vs. NHE) [79], the CB values of CQDs/N-WO3 (−0.38 eV) was more negative, indicating that the O2 molecule can be reduced to O2 •− (Eq.
(3) by photogenerated e − on CB from the catalyst. In contrast, the remainder of the VB was a strongly oxidizing h+, which further oxidized the − OH on the water surface to •OH (Eq.
(4) . Finally, the GAT in the system was further oxidized into intermediates or converted into CO2 and H2O by a large amount of O2 •− and h+, achieving complete mineralization (Eq.
(5) . While for WO3 and N-WO3 system, based on the above discussion in Fig. 3 d, the VB level of WO3 (2.88 eV) had high oxidation potential, which can oxidize H2O and OH− into •OH (E 0(•OH/H2O) = 2.72 eV and E 0(•OH/OH−) = 2.40 eV vs. NHE) [18,40,58]. Nevertheless, the holes at the upward VB position of N-WO3 exhibited a lower oxidation potential of 2.65 eV, and became less likely to react with H2O, which can only oxidize OH− to form •OH. Thus, it was concluded that only •OH was generated for the photocatalytic system corresponding to WO3 and N-WO3, and then the degradation and mineralization of GAT were realized. T These findings were supported by radical quenching experiments and trapping tests. Based on the above discussion, the internal mechanism was deduced that after N doped and CQDs loaded WO3, the band gap was narrower, which increased the utilization rate of visible light and accelerated the separation of photogenerated electrons and holes during the photocatalysis process. Among them, the high electron density of N sites was more conducive to hole capture, and CQDs enhanced electron transport as an electron acceptor and transporter.
(2) CQDs/N-WO3 + hν → CQDs/N-WO3* (photo-excited, h+ + e −)
(3)
e − (CQDs/N-WO3) + O2 → O2 •−
(4) h+ + OH− (surface) → •OH
(5) GAT + O2 •−/h+→ Intermediates → CO2 + H2O

In summary, CQDs/N-WO3 was successfully developed by solvothermal treatment and subsequent calcination method. N-doping and CQDs-loading achieved the modulation of energy bands and electronic structure of WO3, which possessed high photocatalytic activity under simulated solar light irradiation. Particularly, the high electron density at doped N sites was more conducive to hole capture, and the introduction of CQDs brought the W5d orbitals in CQDs/N-WO3 closer to the Fermi level, thereby enhancing electron transfer capability and proximally facilitating photocatalytic reactions. Therefore, after modification, the developed CQDs/N-WO3 exhibited greatly enhanced visible light absorption, narrowed band gap energy, and effective separation of photogenerated charge carriers. The optimized photocatalyst demonstrated excellent photocatalytic performance, achieving a 100 % GAT degradation efficiency and a high k 1 value of 0.055 min−1 at 60 min. Radical quenching experiments and ESR analysis revealed that O2 •− and h+ played the most important role in the CQDs/N-WO3 system. DFT calculation based on the Fukui index further revealed the availability of reactive sites in GAT for electrophilic attack, leading to three degradation pathways for the degradation. The synergistic strategy of doping and loading opens a new perspective for modulating the electronic structure of catalysts, and provides a reference for regulating the ROS types in Fenton-like reactions for the removal of emerging contaminants in water.
